title: Complex analytic vanishing cycles for formal schemes
speaker: Vladimir Berkovich
affiliation: Weizmann Institute of Sciences; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: Let \(R={\cal O}_{{\bf C},0}\) be the ring of power series convergent in a neighborhood of zero in the complex plane. Every scheme \(\cal X\) of finite type over \(R\) defines a complex analytic space \({\cal X}^h\) over an open disc \(D\) of small radius with center at zero. The preimage of the punctured disc \(D^\ast=D\backslash\{0\}\) is denoted by \({\cal X}^h_\eta\), and the preimage of zero coincides with the analytification \({\cal X}_s^h\) of the closed fiber \({\cal X}_s\) of \(\cal X\).

proof_stages.label: Algebraic vanishing-cycle prototype
proof_stages.summary: The talk first recalls Deligne's SGA 7 vanishing-cycle functor for schemes over a Henselian discrete valuation ring, including its definition by pullback to the geometric generic fibre and pushforward to the special fibre and the constructibility theorem for prime-to-residue-characteristic torsion coefficients.

proof_stages.label: Formal-scheme vanishing cycles and formal invariance
proof_stages.summary: Special formal schemes over a discretely valued non-Archimedean field are introduced with their closed and generic fibres, and the formal vanishing-cycle functor is recalled together with its comparison to algebraic vanishing cycles on formal completions, constructibility, functoriality, and a first continuity statement.

proof_stages.label: Complex analytic vanishing cycles
proof_stages.summary: Replacing the geometric generic fibre by the universal cover of a punctured complex disc, the speaker defines analytic vanishing cycles for an analytic space over the disc and a closed subspace of the central fibre, then recalls comparison with algebraic vanishing cycles and algebraic constructibility for finitely generated pi-module local systems.

proof_stages.label: Holomorphic formal vanishing-cycle functor
proof_stages.summary: For a non-Archimedean field K over C with residue field C and pi≅Z inside the absolute Galois group, the main construction is stated: an exact functor from bounded complexes of finitely generated pi-modules to constructible complexes on the analytification of the special fibre of any quasi-compact special formal scheme.

proof_stages.label: Characterizing properties: functoriality, comparisons, locality, tensoring
proof_stages.summary: The new functor is characterized by functoriality in the formal scheme, agreement with ordinary formal-geometry vanishing cycles for finite torsion G-modules, agreement with complex analytic vanishing cycles after choosing a uniformizer, invariance under formal completion along closed subschemes, and a tensor-product formula reducing general coefficients to the integral object.

proof_stages.label: Uniform continuity for regular generic fibres
proof_stages.summary: Under regularity or rig-smoothness assumptions on the generic fibres, congruent morphisms of formal schemes induce the same maps on the constructed vanishing-cycle complexes for all finitely generated pi-module coefficients, giving a stronger continuity result than the earlier torsion statement and a new consequence for analytic vanishing cycles.

proof_stages.label: Integral cohomology of compact non-Archimedean analytic spaces
proof_stages.summary: For a compact strictly K-analytic space, a formal model is chosen and the cohomology of the holomorphic formal vanishing-cycle complex is used to define finitely generated pi-modules that are functorial and model-independent; finite coefficients recover étale cohomology, while integral coefficients give a Z-structure whose l-adic completion recovers l-adic cohomology.

proof_stages.label: Maps to complex cohomology and mixed-Hodge conjecture
proof_stages.summary: The talk ends by describing natural maps between these non-Archimedean integral groups and integral cohomology in complex analytic situations, and conjectures that the groups carry functorial mixed Hodge structures agreeing with limiting mixed Hodge structures for proper schemes.

examples.note: The ring of convergent power series near 0 is used only to make the Henselian discrete valuation ring in the algebraic vanishing-cycle setup concrete.

examples.name: Analytic function germ and Milnor fibre
examples.note: The germ of a holomorphic function near the origin explains why one studies vanishing cycles on a chosen closed subspace, often just the origin, rather than on the whole central fibre, and later illustrates formal-completion dependence and regularity hypotheses.
examples.engagement_reason: he specializes the analytic vanishing-cycle setup to a germ of an analytic function, taking a small ball mapping to the disc and the point 0 as the relevant closed subspace/Milnor-fibre stalk

passes.reason: An analytic function germ with its Milnor fibre is presented as a typical source of vanishing cycles.

examples.name: Closed-point restriction in a one-parameter analytic family
examples.note: A finite-type family over the local ring of the complex line is used to illustrate the locality theorem: vanishing cycles restricted to a closed point depend only on the formal completion at that point.
examples.engagement_reason: he applies the restriction theorem to the closed-point case in a one-parameter analytic family, identifying the restricted vanishing cycles with those from the formal completion at that point

examples.name: Proper scheme over K as a compact analytic space
examples.note: Analytifying a proper K-scheme gives a compact strictly K-analytic space, showing that the constructed integral groups recover l-adic cohomology after tensoring and are expected to carry the limiting mixed Hodge structure.
examples.engagement_reason: he treats a proper scheme over K as a compact analytic space via analytification and uses comparison to identify the resulting groups with étale cohomology, deriving the integral-structure conclusion
